title: Quiz: QAOA and Max-Cut Algorithms
description: Test your understanding of QAOA for Max-Cut and combinatorial optimization

questions.question: The Max-Cut cost function for a partition z in {-1, +1}^n is encoded as which Hamiltonian?

options.text: H_C = sum_{(i,j) in E} w_{ij} * Z_i * Z_j
H_C = sum_{(i,j) in E} w_{ij} * (I - Z_i * Z_j) / 2
H_C = sum_{(i,j) in E} w_{ij} * X_i * X_j

questions.explanation: The Max-Cut cost Hamiltonian is H_C = sum w_{ij}(I - Z_i Z_j)/2. This correctly encodes the cut: when z_i = z_j (same partition), Z_i Z_j = +1 and the term contributes 0; when z_i != z_j (different partitions), Z_i Z_j = -1 and the term contributes w_{ij}.

questions.question: In QAOA, what is the role of the mixer Hamiltonian H_M = sum_i X_i?

options.text: It measures the qubits in the computational basis
It creates transitions between computational basis states

questions.explanation: The mixer Hamiltonian generates transitions between different computational basis states (candidate solutions). When applied as exp(-i*beta*H_M), it allows exploration of the solution space by mixing amplitudes between states that differ by single bit flips.

questions.question: The initial state for standard QAOA is |+>^n. Why is this choice important?

options.text: It is the ground state of the cost Hamiltonian
It is the ground state of the mixer Hamiltonian H_M = sum_i X_i
It is required for quantum error correction

questions.explanation: |+>^n is the ground state of -H_M (equivalently, the maximum eigenstate of H_M). Starting from this state ensures smooth interpolation: at beta = infinity, the mixer dominates and we stay at |+>^n; as beta decreases and gamma increases, we transition toward low-energy states of H_C.

questions.question: For p=1 QAOA on 3-regular graphs, the worst-case approximation ratio is approximately:
questions.explanation: For p=1 QAOA on 3-regular graphs, the worst-case approximation ratio is approximately 0.6924. This is worse than the classical Goemans-Williamson algorithm (0.878) but better than random guessing (0.5). Higher values of p improve this bound.

questions.question: How is the ZZ interaction exp(-i*gamma*Z_i*Z_j) implemented in a quantum circuit?

options.text: A single RZZ gate between qubits i and j
RZ(gamma) on qubit i followed by RZ(gamma) on qubit j
Hadamard gates on both qubits followed by CNOT

questions.explanation: The ZZ rotation exp(-i*gamma*Z_i*Z_j) is implemented using two CNOT gates with an RZ rotation in between: CNOT(i,j) maps Z_j to Z_i*Z_j, then RZ(2*gamma) applies the rotation, and the second CNOT unmaps back. This uses the identity that CNOT conjugates Z on the target to ZZ.

questions.question: As the number of QAOA layers p approaches infinity, the algorithm is guaranteed to find the optimal Max-Cut solution.
questions.explanation: True. As p -> infinity, QAOA can approximate any unitary evolution, including the one that projects onto the optimal solution. This is because the cost and mixer unitaries together generate the full unitary group. However, finding the optimal parameters becomes increasingly difficult.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'warm-starting' QAOA?

options.text: To reduce the number of qubits needed
To initialize the quantum state biased toward a classical solution

questions.explanation: Warm-starting QAOA initializes the quantum state biased toward a good classical solution (e.g., from a heuristic algorithm). Instead of starting from the uniform superposition |+>^n, the initial state has higher amplitude on states close to the classical solution, potentially allowing QAOA to find better solutions more quickly.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid strategies for QAOA parameter optimization? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Grid search over all parameter combinations

questions.explanation: Layerwise training (b), gradient-based optimization (c), and random restarts with local optimization (d) are all practical strategies. Grid search (a) becomes impractical for QAOA because the number of parameters grows linearly with p, making exhaustive search exponentially expensive in p.
